speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six. January 24th, 1996, and our survivor is Rabbi Nathan Lipschitz. Could you tell me about your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. What would you like to know?

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they born and their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Their names-- the older ones-- I have three children, two-- two boys and one girl. And the two boys are married and the older one his name is Judah Lipschitz, or Yehuda Lipschitz. Lives in Israel now, has three children up to last night and from last night there is four, the fourth one, a boy after three girls. And they live in Israel and the oldest one name is [? Dassi. ?] And she's about 16 years old. The second daughter is Mirl, and she's about going to be 14. And the third one is Faigie, [INAUDIBLE]. She is about four. And the one was just now born to [INAUDIBLE], and we don't know yet a name yet because he was just born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is Yehuda's wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yehuda's wife, maiden name is [INAUDIBLE]. Born in Israel. She has parents in Israel. And she's a very nice wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: First name is Frieda.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your second child?

speaker: interviewee
text: My second child is Yitzhak or Ira in English. And he is married with a wife Esther, which [? he ?] just passed here. Her maiden name is [? Gerstner. ?] Her father's a lawyer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they have four children. The first one is Breindy, and she is about 10 years old. The second one is a boy, Joshie. He's named after my brother, [INAUDIBLE]. And he is seven going on eight years old. How old? Yes. And the third one is Ayala. Hinda Ayala is her name, and she is six, I think, six years old. And the youngest one is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? Donna-- ?] [? Donna ?] we call her, and she is about five.

speaker: interviewee
text: These are the four children. This son doesn't live here in Brooklyn. He lives in Passaic, New Jersey and he is in the carpet business. And I have another son. The third one is my daughter [? Casey, ?] and she's still single, a little choosy. And I hope someday pretty soon the right one, the-- the-- right one will come and somehow I'll get rid of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about your religious involvements with the community now? What organizations you belong to or shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: I go to a shul in the neighborhood. And I don't belong only to the [INAUDIBLE] shul I belong. And I am pretty happy, and I'm also a member in the rabbinical organization. It's called [INAUDIBLE]. I'm a member there. And my activities are not that big. I like very much to do something. I do some with the Russian Jews because I keep on remembering when I was a refugee. So whatever I can here and there, I do for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any hobbies now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hobbies? Russian Jews is a hobby. And I like silver a lot, so I do a little business with silver. But my hobby, my love, is the Judaica, especially those made by hand by very important artists. And I have some stuff from them, originals only that I have in some cases, and from the greatest artists they are in this field now. They are in Israel, and I was in Israel I bought from them. Sometimes one of them comes here. And this is mainly a hobby. Here and there I sell. I make a few dollars also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever return to your hometown in Poland after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 11 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is it like? Very difficult to describe. I would have to have much, much more command in English language. And but to tell it in a very simple form as a layman, it was like you never or for years you never had a drop of water or have anything to do with the water, and then you come in and you pour it over something that makes you like little holes in the body all over. I went-- first of all, I didn't recognize it. When we came, we came-- we came to [? check this ?] from Krakow, I didn't recognize it. Was a funny thing happened. I found out later, I asked what happened. I was in a few towns and the same thing-- the ring, which is the circle of the town which almost every town, small or large, had it in Poland, all of them that I have seen are no more circles but it's made like gardens-- trees and flowers and seeds and benches. I didn't-- therefore for this was the heart of that little town. The same thing, I was in my zayde's town and-- and Bochnia and another town, the same thing go on. I didn't understand it. Since when are they so good, have so much money to make-- I found out later what it was, because it was under Communism. The whole center, the nerve of life of every town, was that circle. The farmers came in and whatever were-- now with Communism, there was no stores, hardly any stores. There was no business, no selling or buying. That looked like a cemetery. And the people came into that place, they were like feeling an act and then our government could not allow this to happen to-- to-- because they were afraid that it would give a bad feeling and whatever the consequences could be of that. Well, they don't wait-- so they decided to fix it that way. And then every town I saw was-- so I didn't recognize my little town. But then finally standing and looking around, slowly, slowly I came to myself and it wasn't very much emotion in the beginning. But when I came to the near where we were, our little town is, and a few Polacks and I see the shul is not there and our house isn't there, I broke out crying that some of the Polish came over and pat me I should stop because I couldn't control. Now, I can't tell you what that feeling was. The feeling was that a world comes to an end. That I wasn't even angry then, but there was a different feeling like I feel that the world ends. And I was looking where every house that I recognized who lived there. It was just like every time I looked, like-- like a piece of-- a-- a little more of you was-- was broken off. Unbelievable, especially the beis medrash and the shul. And I saw-- and then I went to the cemetery. My mother died before the war. She lays there. And there wasn't a sign of a cemetery. There was a woman who her father before the war was paid by the Jewish community to take care of the cemetery, to clean the grass and so forth. He is not alive no more, and she had some cows there on the grass of the cemetery. There was no more fences, there was no, not one stone around. All them had saved is the stones. I was told that like in other towns, they took the stones into their construction, to-- to put near the house to make there shouldn't be no wet. And not one stone. However, they-- this woman I-- showed me before the cemetery, before it was a cemetery-- I knew more or less. Of course, I was raised there. And there was a grass and but you saw that they had boards underneath the grass. You see it's not like round. It's straight. So I knew that happened but she showed me that there is that-- that when one day the Germans in their occupation, they decided that they got-- get rid of the old people so they didn't even want them schlepping to camps, the old people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took about I think 48-- 47, 48 people, and they brought them up, and she told me she was standing there. Brought them up on the cemetery, and they dug a big hole. There was that hole that was covered now. A big hole, and they're about to shoot them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somehow there was between them some young men from the shtetl. They didn't know their name. I knew everybody, didn't know the name. Who was also there between those to be shot. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they were all undressed already to be shot. Then all of a sudden, they bring a kid to put between the people to be shot, one kid. That guy, that young fellow who was must have done something that they want to got so [NON-ENGLISH], so angry that he attacked the German. He grabbed the gun from him, and start shooting at him. He ran away. She remembered. She was a kid. The woman told me, she was a kid. The-- the-- the German went into their house to hide, and he shot at him. He gave him a little, he wounded him. And in their house, he washed-- he went in before he reached the house, and he came in there and washed this blood off. And apparently the funny part about it-- funny I don't know you can anything funny there, that the guy talked to them, to this Polish. And then he said, I didn't know that there are Jews that can do such a-- a thing. From now on I'll respect them. He-- he was touched by this that he got so aggravated. Of course, they took him back-- he went back himself. He knew he doesn't have a chance, and they shot them all and buried them in that spot. So me and my family, we were saying a little [NON-ENGLISH] and we were crying and so on. We left. And the Polish people-- there was one that recognized me, although he was almost 50 years because I used to-- he was the mailman. He was also a carpenter, so he used to be very often our house. We got the most mail. Very nice [? goy, ?] a very nice fellow. So he recognized me, he was so happy talk to me a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the others, they were told who I am so they knew, mostly younger people, and what they told me, no, why don't you come back to live here? You were born and raised here. Why don't you come back? Then they ask me-- they said a few pieces that happened. They saw that I translated to my wife and children-- we were all together then. They translate, and they ask me, how come your-- your family doesn't know Polish? You are a real Pole. I tell them, what? I got so angry the second or the third time, I'm a real Pole? I says, I left this town when I was over 21. And since I remember all the time here and there I was hit in the head and all I heard is [POLISH]. It mean, 'Jew go to Israel, go to Palestine.' That-- I wasn't a Polack then-- then a Pole. Now I'm a Pole all of a sudden? How is that-- I'll say no more. Then the same thing. I were-- I was in Krakow in the hotel. Also some guy there spoke in Polish. I speak pretty good, but here and there some words are missing because I don't speak in so long. So also the same thing. How come your family doesn't-- doesn't speak Polish? After all, you're a real Pole. Real Polish. So the same thing. It's-- they-- there was some, was a Polish man begged me. I wanted to stay a little longer. Here, I'll take you in to sleep, couldn't do it. But surprisingly enough, a few-- I had-- we had a maid I told you, an older [? goy. ?] I was looking for her and she is not alive no more, and she had two sons. They're not alive no more. There was one son's wife left though, so I gave-- I gave a few people a little money there. And they told, [? you know, ?] that I'm the big American, and they started to try and-- he did-- he did--

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to them, I want one thing. There was over 100 families-- houses, living quarters. Give me a picture from somebody, give me a-- a piece or a silver candle. I'll light it here. Give me anything. I'll pay. Nothing. Nobody wanted to say anything. They had it all. They had it all. The Germans took everything. The Germans took everything. We didn't do anything at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we left with a broken heart. I don't know how. You know something? We say a broken heart, sounds like a broken glass, but a broken glass can only get broken once. And how many times can a heart get broken? I really don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we left back to Krakow. We went to different cemeteries that I had some grandfather, some from the dynasty there. And we went. We cried here. We cried here in the cemetery. We were there only a few days. And we left. I went to Israel from there. So there was a little-- little salve a little to-- to-- to cover the wound for-- for a little while. [INAUDIBLE] My son already [? always ?] had already lived in Israel. I send him tickets to come when we went. This-- this is the story about, in short, in condensed form, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: What achievements or accomplishments are you most proud of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very, very proud of my children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's no use going into details. Everybody more or less feels like this, but this is achievement. It went that way. I-- I don't know if-- I don't consider it achievement. I didn't discover anything. Or I didn't develop any big industries or very big charitable organizations. No, none of that. But I'm proud of my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think gave you the strength to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: For better times and one very important thing, to see that the enemy-- to live to a day that the enemy gets paid at least a part of it. That's number one. And number two, the great hope to see that the Jews are rebuilt themselves, rebuilt families, rebuilt-- [CRYING] [SPEAKING YIDDISH] My heart was growing, and I was standing sometimes and watching cheders, yeshivas, and-- and-- and [NON-ENGLISH], and this gave me the-- the-- and I saw how it get stronger and bigger and more people who-- who had so much in for the-- for God. And slowly, slowly they made peace, and they stop blaming God. And-- but this is a side thing that it's too much has do with a soul. I don't want to-- I-- I can't step on those-- on those things. But just to see that those little leftover coals from the fire are rebuilding and are rebuilding families and some are successful in one way, some are-- this gave me the strength. And, of course, you have children, you have loving children, so your life is to see that it multiplies and see that they get the best what you can provide. This is a part of life. This is-- this is what gives you the-- the strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have dreams or nightmares as a result?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, are you kidding? [LAUGHS] I was with the Germans only two weeks, and the frightenings-- frightenings from them was only that time, that day that I tell you, and on the road going back. But I still have dreams that the Germans come in, they-- I hide and I see their boots. Only in two weeks I was there, until this day, and I wake up with sweat. And the fear is so bad sometimes, I feel my heart later. Until this day. Of course, as-- as time progresses its get stretched, but it still is here. Sometimes only fragments of it and sometime I see a full-- the Germans come in, and they're looking for me, and I'm hiding. Things that I didn't even go through, only from reading or-- or hearing, and it's just like-- it happens to me now. All the time. Not all the time. A lot of times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What advice do you have for your children and your grandchildren, for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: To keep on doing what they're doing and don't stop and don't get frightened or discouraged from people or even enemies that-- that threaten you or give you-- or you hear bad news. Keep on doing and don't get stopped because there wasn't any people in the world that went through what we went through in the Hitler era and somehow we survived. That doesn't mean that 6 million got killed. There is no words to-- to talk about this now, but the fact is that the Jewish people survived. That he wanted to destroy completely all the Jewish nation. He-- this he couldn't reach, fortunately, because there were so many Jews [? other ?] other countries. But even us who really destroyed, he didn't destroy us fully. And it's not much of a-- of a compensation, but it's enough to have the courage to go on and to tell your generations, give them the courage to keep on struggling, going on, and not get frightened. Because the fear is-- there wasn't any more fear than I had at certain times with them, and somehow I lived through it and I keep-- I keep going. Life goes on. So this is the only thing that-- that I can tell them. These-- these two things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you now-- now like to deliver to the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be honest with you, if I tell you a message to the world according to what the world likes to hear, would be good words like we hear from Anne-- Anne Frank. You know that why she got so popular because she said they're still [? hope and ?] that people are generally good. I cannot say that because the way it's going on, the people are killing each other, and it's going on after 50 years. They are still going on and people from the same nations, from the same place, here and there they said because we're hated because we are Jews. Now it goes on not between Jews and goes on. To keep on going and do the best everybody can. And I'm not that great and that smart to say something original that the world will swallow up and take a lesson.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what can I tell you? We are here and we are alive. We should make the best of it and see that we, as much as we can, even not only spiritually even physically just because of Jew, if you can enjoy life-- I'm talking about normal, within the [NON-ENGLISH]. Just-- [NON-ENGLISH] You know for-- you know what that means? You want to explain it?

speaker: interviewee
text: To spite our enemies and not to be afraid what this one will say or this gentile would say, because they'll say it anyway and if they have a chance, those that hate us have a chance they'll do a bad-- whatever they can. So we have to-- we shouldn't be afraid and go our ways, and when it comes up fight it, but not to be afraid because this one will say or that one will say. Even if this is a plain worker or this intelligent man or this is a diplomat. Don't mean nothing. When they'll have a chance, those enemies, they'll do it anyway. And we have to do our part when it comes up to fight it. Otherwise, not to be afraid and not to try to be nice in their eyes. Of course we have to be-- to try to do the best we can, but not for them. Only for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you on behalf of the Shoah Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Don't keep me keep me here, because I'm already bursting out. This is my grandfather, my father's father. He was a rabbi. Lived in Brigel, Brzesko in the Polish name for the town. And he was during the war for awhile under Russian occupation in Lviv and then in Peremyshliany. And then he was with very difficulties with a-- with a coal truck, transferred back to Brigel where he lived originally. And then he was sent into Bochnia ghetto where he was killed and burned in the middle of-- in the street. He was called the Wielopole rabbi. His name was Yitzchak Lipschitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you'll identify.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is a picture of the girls, mostly teenagers, who were in a class of a Bais Yaakov that was organized a few years before the war in our little town of Wielopole. And these are most the girls of the whole town by that-- that-- in that age-- that were all. And there is-- I keep that picture. I was very happy to get it that my sister is there. And from here she is about the fifth from the left side with braids, blonde hair with braids. This is my sister Alte. And they were other-- and the rest is other kids from the shtetl. Most of them I know. There's only one that I know that is still alive from this group, and she lives here in Far Rockaway. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] All kids that I still recognize, most of them at least. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is the one on the bottom that's still alive. Her name is [? Hannah ?] [INAUDIBLE]. Well, this is a picture when my sister Hinda got married, and due to the fact that my father was the rabbi and my grandfather was the Hasidic rabbi was probably 1,000 followers, it was a very, very festive thing. And the people in town wanted to make it more festive as more-- as much as possible, so a group of Jewish fellas-- some older, some younger-- put uniforms on like [NON-ENGLISH] and on horses. Those that could ride horses, and they went to receive the [INAUDIBLE], who came from another town with a train through Ropczyce. And from Ropczyce, they-- they received them because they were the train-- the last stop of the train. And our town was no train. So they came with this-- with the horses to receive [? her, ?] and this was a very festive thing. A matter of fact, then the-- the Polish people were making a sort of demonstration in middle of the road. They couldn't digest this, that there's such a-- a great festivity with-- with the wedding. They were-- a matter of fact, they were maybe-- maybe even a few hundred people from the towns around that were not even invited and came to see the town, because it was what's called a [NON-ENGLISH]. And this is the-- there's, as far as I know, there's one alive. As a matter of fact, the guy that called me from Israel to wish me mazel tov, he is here too. And he's the only one that I think is alive. There was another one that is here, not on a horse then the-- that was alive but died only about a year ago. And my brother's picture is there too that stays between the horses, but it's too small to-- to point him out. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No, that's the one that I just told you died not long ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: There it is. You see? In the middle holding the-- the flag, this is my-- my brother, Chaim [? Myer. ?]
